chunk_summary: Guidance on complementary feeding for 6–24 months, emphasizing age-appropriate textures, meal frequency, dietary diversity, and safety to support growth, neurodevelopment, and infection prevention.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of complementary feeding during the 6–24 month period for growth and development.
Learn how to counsel caregivers on age-appropriate textures, meal frequency, and dietary diversity.
Identify risks such as iron deficiency anemia, unsafe food storage, and puree dependence beyond 10 months.
Apply global recommendations for 6–23 months to the entire 6–24 month period in clinical practice.
key_facts: Complementary feeding begins when milk alone no longer meets nutrient needs, typically after 6 months.
Global guidelines focus on 6–23 months, but evidence can be applied to the 6–24 month period.
Goals include age-appropriate textures, adequate meal frequency, energy density, and daily micronutrient-rich foods.
Breastfeeding can continue up to 2 years or beyond, but complementary foods must provide most nutrients after the first year.
In India, growth faltering often accelerates during the transition to family foods due to thin, cereal-heavy, unhygienic meals.
Clinic notes should separate feeding domains to prevent missed diagnoses like iron deficiency anemia or recurrent diarrhea.
reference_quote: Global recommendations and measurement indicators are usually written for 6–23 months, while many clinical schedules describe 'under two years' (through 24 months).
